subjects will be
taken up: if
"Natural
Sciences," the
branches should
be stated.

MEMORANDUM REGARDING THE POSITION OF OFFICERS TO BE APPOINTED
TO HER MAJESTY'S INDIAN MEDICAL SERVICE.

India Office, September, 1898.

1. This memorandum is based on the regulations in force at the present time. They are subject to any alteration that may be determined on.

PASSAGE TO INDIA.

2. Officers on appointment are, when possible, provided with passage to India by troop ship; when troop ship accommodation is not available, passage at the public expense is provided by steamer, or a passage allowance granted, if preferred. A charge for messing during the voyage is made at the rate of 2s. a day. This payment does not include the cost of liquors, which are charged for as extras.

3. Any officer who may neglect or refuse to proceed to India under the orders of the Secretary of State for India within two months from the date of leaving Netley, will be considered as having forfeited his appointment, unless special circumstances shall justify a departure from this regulation.

PAY PREVIOUS TO ARRIVAL IN INDIA.

4. The rate of pay drawn by Lieutenants from the date of passing the final examination at the Army Medical School until arrival in India is 10s. a day. (For rates subsequent to their landing in India, see paras. 16, 17, and 18.)

Pay at the above rate is issued in this country up to the date of embarkation, and an advance of two months' pay at the same rate is also made prior to embarkation, which is adjusted in India in accordance with the rate laid down in para. 16.

GRADES AND PRECEDENCE.

5. The grades of Officers in the Indian Medical Service are six in number, viz. :—

1. Surgeon-General. 4. Major.
2. Colonel. 5. Captain.
3. Lieutenant-Colonel. 6. Lieutenant.

PROMOTION.

6. A Lieutenant's Commission dates from the day of passing out of the Army Medical School.

7. A Lieutenant is promoted to Captain on completion of three years' full-pay* service from date of first Commission.

8. A Captain is promoted to Major on completion of 12 years' full-pay* service.

9. A Major is promoted to Lieutenant-Colonel on completion of 20 years' full-pay* service.

10. All promotions from the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel to that of Colonel, and from the rank of Colonel to that of Surgeon-General, are given by selection for ability and merit.

11. On appointment as Honorary Physician or Honorary Surgeon to Her Majesty an Officer below the rank of Colonel is promoted to that rank, remaining supernumerary until absorbed.

12. For distinguished service in the field an Officer of the Indian Medical Service may be promoted from any rank to that next above it, remaining supernumerary in the higher rank until he would have been promoted in ordinary course, or until selected for further promotion for which he is eligible according to his standing in the higher rank.

* See, however, para. 41.

TENURE OF OFFICE IN ADMINISTRATIVE GRADES.

13. The tenure of office of Surgeon-Generals and Colonels is limited to five years.
14. Colonels, if not disqualified by age, are eligible either for employment for a second tour of duty in the same grade, or for employment in the higher grade of Surgeon-General by promotion thereto.
15. Absence on leave in excess of eight months during a five years' tour of duty involves forfeiture of appointment.

PAY AND ALLOWANCES.

16. The following are the rates of "Indian pay and allowances" drawn by officers of the Indian Medical Service:—

Rank. Years' Service. Indian Pay and Allowances per mensem.
When not holding an appointment carrying higher pay.
Lieutenant-Colonel (hitherto ranking as Brigade-Surgeon-Lieutenant-Colonel) ... .. R. A. P.
Lieutenant-Colonel ... .. 25 and over 900 0 0
Major " ... .. 20 " 888 12 0
" ... .. 15 " 852 3 7
" ... .. 12 " 677 6 11
640 14 6
A. B.
When not receiving any Staff Allowance. When receiving Staff Allowance in addition. (See para. 18 and note.)
Captain ... .. 10 " R. A. P. R. A. P.
" ... .. 5 " 500 0 0 450 0 0
Captain and Lieutenant ... under 5 450 0 0 400 0 0
350 0 0 300 0 0

17. On first appointment they only come into receipt of "Indian pay and allowances" (Column A) from the date of their arrival in India.

18. Officers holding the principal administrative appointments and substantive military charges of the Indian Medical Service receive at present the following consolidated salaries:—

Rs. per mensem.
Surgeon-General ... .. \left\{ \begin{array}{l} 1 @ \dots 2,700 \\ 2 @ \dots 2,500 \\ 2 @ \dots 2,200 \\ \text{from} \dots 1,800 \\ \text{to} \dots 2,250 \end{array} \right.
Colonel ... ..
Lieutenant-Colonel in substantive charge* of a Native regiment ... .. 1,100 or 1,000, with Rs. 90 horse allowance in Cavalry regiments.

* Medical charges are of two kinds—substantive and officiating. The first is held by an officer as his fixed and permanent appointment. For this he is entitled to the monthly consolidated pay laid down in the above scale (para. 19), subject to the condition specified in para. 23.

An officiating charge is held by an officer who is acting temporarily in an appointment during the absence of the Officer whose substantive charge it is. The officer thus officiating receives not the consolidated pay of the appointment laid down in the scale (para. 19), but the Indian pay and allowances (para. 17) of his own grade plus half the staff pay of the appointment in which he is officiating plus half the staff pay of his own appointment, if he have one).

The staff pay of an appointment is the difference between the Indian pay and allowances and the consolidated pay of the permanent incumbent. It has been ruled, however, that the extra allowance for an officiating charge shall never be less than Rs. 100 per mensem.

Example 1. A Captain or Lieutenant of less than 5 years' service officiates for one of the same period of service in charge of a regiment. He receives Rs. 300 + Rs. 100 = Rs. 400.

Example 3. A Captain of 5 years' service holding permanent medical charge of a regiment, and officiating for a Major over 15 years' service absent from his regiment, receives Rs. 400 + Rs. 800 — Rs. 677. 6. 11 + Rs. 600 — Rs. 400 = Rs. 561. 4. 6.

PAY AND ALLOWANCES—continued.

Rs. per
mensem.
Major in substantive charge of a Native regiment ... 800 with Rs. 90 horse allowance in Cavalry regiments.
Captain above 5 years' full-pay service in substantive charge of a Native regiment ... 600 with Rs. 60 ditto.
Captain or Lieutenant under 5 years' ditto in substantive charge of a Native regiment ... 450 with Rs. 60 ditto.

19. The salaries of other substantive medical appointments in the Civil and Military Departments are consolidated, and vary from Rs. 400 to Rs. 1,800 per mensem.

20. Qualified Officers of the Medical Service are also eligible for appointments in the Assay Department. The salaries of these appointments are from Rs. 600 to Rs. 2,250 per mensem.

21. Officers are required to perform two years' regimental duty in India before they can be considered eligible for civil employment.

22. Except in the administrative grades, and in certain special appointments, medical officers are not debarred from taking private practice, so long as it does not interfere with their proper duties.

23. No officer, however employed, can receive any staff allowance in addition to the pay laid down in para. 16 unless he has passed the examination in Hindustani known as the "Lower Standard." The passing of this examination does not of itself bring any increase of pay to an officer, unless appointed to a substantive or officiating charge; but failure to pass disqualifies an officer, even when holding such substantive or officiating charge, from receiving any portion of the staff allowances of the appointment.

24. Surgeon-Generals and Colonels, on vacating office at the expiration of the five years' tour of duty, are permitted to draw in India respectively an unemployed salary of Rs. 1,200 per mensem in the former, and Rs. 900 in the latter case, for a period of six months from the date of their vacating office, after which they are placed while unemployed on the following scale of pay:—

Surgeon-General.
Per diem.		 Colonel.
Per diem.
£ s. d. £ s. d.
After 30 years' service on full pay ... 2 5 0 1 14 0
" 25 " " ... 2 5 0 1 10 0
" 20 " " , or on promotion,
should this period of service not be completed ...		 2 0 0 1 8 0

LEAVE RULES.

(Paras. 25 (2) to 29 apply only to officers in Military employ.)

25. Officers of the Indian Medical Service, below the rank of Colonel may be granted:—

(1.) Privilege leave under such regulations as may from time to time be in force.

(2.) Leave out of India, for no longer period than one year, capable of extension to two years' absence from duty, on the following pay:—

£
After arrival in India, on first appointment ... 200 a year.
After the commencement of the 5th year's service for pension ... 250 "
Ditto, ditto, 10th ditto ... 300 "
Ditto, ditto, 15th ditto ... 400 "
Ditto, ditto, 20th ditto ... 450 "
Ditto, ditto, 25th ditto ... 500 "

(3.) Leave in India, but for the period of one year only, on full military pay and half the Staff salary appointment.

26. No extension of leave involving absence from duty for more than two years, whether taken in or out of India, can be granted except on specially urgent grounds and without pay.

27. An officer unable on account of the state of his health to return to duty within the maximum period of two years' absence, unless he is under para. 26 specially granted an extension of leave without pay, is placed on temporary half-pay or the retired list, as the circumstances of the case may require. An officer is also liable to be placed on half-pay or the retired list should his health require an undue amount of leave, whether in or out of India.

28. Leave may be granted at any time, but solely at the discretion of the Civil or Military Authorities in India under whom an officer may be serving.

LEAVE RULES—continued.

29. An officer on leave, whether in India or out of India, is required to rejoin at once on being recalled to duty, unless certified by a Medical Board as unfit to do so.

30. Officers of the Administrative Grades may be granted one period of leave not exceeding eight months during their tenure of appointment.

HONOURS AND REWARDS.

31. Officers of the Indian Medical Service are eligible for the military distinction of the Order of the Bath, and for other orders, British and Indian, and for good service pensions.

Six of the most meritorious officers are named Honorary Physicians, and six are named Honorary Surgeons to Her Majesty.

RETIRING PENSIONS AND HALF-PAY.

32. Officers of the Indian Medical Service are allowed to retire on the following scale of pension, on completion of the required periods of service:—

Per annum.
£
After 30 years' service for pension ... ... ... ... ... ... 700
" 25 " " " " ... ... 500
" 20 " " " " ... ... 365
" 17 " " " " ... ... 292

33. Service for pension reckons from date of first commission, and includes all leave taken under the rules quoted in paras. 25 to 30. (See also para 41.)

34. A Surgeon-General, after five years' active employment in India in that appointment, is entitled to retire upon a pension of £350 per annum, in addition to that to which he may be entitled under the above scale.

35. A Colonel is entitled, after five years' active employment in India in that appointment, to retire upon a pension of £250 per annum, in addition to the pension to which he may be entitled under the above scale.

36. In each of the above cases stated in paras. 34 and 35 eight months' absence on leave is allowed to count towards actual service in those grades. (See para. 30.)

37. A Surgeon-General or Colonel who has completed his term of service and has reverted to British pay may reside in Europe, at the same time qualifying for higher pension.

38. With a view to maintain the efficiency of the service, all officers of the rank of Lieutenant-Colonel or Major are placed on the Retired List when they have attained the age of 55 years, and all Surgeon-Generals and Colonels when they have attained the age of 60 years. In any special case, where it would appear to be for the good of the service that the officer should continue in employment, he may be so continued, subject in each case to the sanction of the Secretary of State for India in Council.

39. Officers not entitled to pension, when placed on temporary or permanent half-pay under para. 27 are granted the British rate of half-pay of their military rank, as under:—

Rank. Rates of Half-Pay.
Per Diem. Per Annum.
Lieutenant-Colonel ... .. s. d.
11 0		 £ s. d.
200 15 0
Major ... .. 9 6 173 7 6
Captain ... .. 7 0 127 15 0
Lieutenant ... .. 3 0 54 15 0

Officers cannot retire in India on half pay (No. 45, 28th February, 1856).

INVALID PENSIONS.

40. An Officer who has become incapacitated for further service in India on account of unfitness caused by duty may, after he has been two years on temporary half-pay, be granted Invalid Pension on the following scale:—

Per Annum.
After 16 years' pension service... ... ... ... ... ... ... ... £272
" 15 " " " " " " " " 252
" 14 " " " " " " " " 232
" 13 " " " " " " " " 212
" 12 " " " " " " " " 192

41. Time (not exceeding one year) passed on temporary half-pay reckons as service for promotion and pension, in the case of an officer placed on half-pay on account of ill-health contracted in the performance of military duty.

42. Officers of the Indian Medical Service are liable, after retirement on pension before completing 30 years' service, to recall to military duty in case of any great emergency arising, up to 55 years of age.

WOUND PENSIONS.

43. Officers are entitled to the same allowances on account of wounds and injuries received in action as are granted to combatant officers of Her Majesty's Indian Military Forces holding the corresponding military rank.

FAMILY PENSIONS.

44. The claims to pension of widows and families of officers are treated under the provisions of such Royal Warrant regulating the grant of pensions to the widows and families of British officers as may be in force at the time being.

45. The widows and families of officers are also entitled to pensions under the Indian Service Family Pension Regulations, for the benefits of which all officers must, as a condition of their appointment, subscribe from the date of their arrival in India.

LEAVE RULES FOR DEPUTY COMMISSARIES, ASSISTANT COMMISSARIES AND DEPUTY ASSISTANT COMMISSARIES, SENIOR ASSISTANT-SURGEONS AND WARRANT OFFICERS OF THE INDIAN ARMY.

Note.—By Royal Warrant dated 20th Nov., 1897, the grades of Deputy Commissary, Assistant Commissary, and Deputy Assistant Commissaries were granted commissions as such, with honorary rank as follows:—

Deputy Commissary ... ... ... ... ... Captain.
Assistant Commissary ... ... ... ... ... } Lieutenant.
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... ... ... ... ...

(Published in India on 8th November, 1899.)

1. These rules shall apply to—

(a) All combatants and medical warrant officers of the Indian Army promoted to warrant rank on or after the date of their publication.

(b) Deputy commissaries, assistant commissaries, and deputy assistant commissaries, senior assistant-surgeons, and warrant officers who elected them within one year of the date of their publication.

2. Those who apply for leave of any kind within one year of the date of publication of these rules must make their election when submitting their applications for leave; all others, whether on duty or on furlough or leave on the date of publication of these rules, may elect at convenience within the specified time.

3. All deputy commissaries, assistant commissaries, deputy assistant commissaries, senior assistant-surgeons, and warrant officers who elect these rules will come under their provisions from the date of the receipt of their applications at Army Headquarters, or by the Governments of India, Madras, or Bombay, or at the India Office if at home; and they will be entitled to count all past furlough and leave as service for pension under Rule V.

4. Those who elect these rules while on furlough or leave under older regulations will be allowed the option of continuing to draw any furlough or leave allowances they may be in receipt of under those regulations, if more advantageous, until the expiry of such furlough or leave or any extension thereof, subject to the limit of two years' absence from duty.

5. It must be clearly understood that deputy commissaries, assistant commissaries, deputy assistant commissaries, senior assistant-surgeons, or warrant officers who do not elect these rules within the period stated will be held to have declined them, and will be allowed no further opportunity of electing them.

RULES.

I. A deputy commissary, assistant commissary, deputy assistant commissary, senior assistant-surgeon, or warrant officer subject to these rules may be granted—

(a) Privilege leave under such regulations as may from time to time be in force.

(b) Leave in or out of India for one year, capable of extension to two years' absence from duty under conditions stated below.

II. During any time within one year spent on

leave in India other than privilege leave a deputy commissary, assistant commissary, deputy assistant commissary, or combatant warrant officer will draw the warrant pay and allowances of his rank and the half staff salary of his appointment, or, if the pay of his appointment be a consolidated salary, then the warrant pay and allowances of his rank and half the difference between such pay and allowances and his consolidated salary; and within the same period a member of the Subordinate Medical Department will draw three-fourths of his grade pay and of his house rent. After one year, and up to the limit of two years, an honorary commissioned officer or combatant warrant officer will draw half of the warrant pay and allowances of his rank and of the staff salary of his appointment, or half of his consolidated salary, as the case may be; and a member of the Indian Subordinate Medical Department will draw half of his grade pay and of his house rent.

During leave out of India the following rates of pay will be drawn:—

Per annum.
£
Deputy Commissary ... .. 210
Assistant ... .. 150
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... .. 110
Conductor ... .. 78
Sub-Conductor ... .. 72
Senior Assistant-Surgeon, with the honorary rank of Surgeon-Captain 170
Senior Assistant-Surgeon, with the honorary rank of Surgeon-Lieutenant ... .. 120
Assistant-Surgeon, 1st Class, above 5 years' service as such ... .. 85
Assistant-Surgeon, 1st Class, under 5 years' service as such ... .. 65
Assistant-Surgeon, 2nd Class, above 5 years' service as such ... .. 50
Assistant-Surgeon, 2nd Class, under 5 years' service as such ... .. 40
Assistant-Surgeon, 3rd Class ... .. 30

III. Leave may be granted at any time at the discretion of the Commanders-in-Chief of the several Armies, the Governor-General in Council, or the Governors in Council, as the case may be.

IV. Leave in or out of India in excess of one year cannot be claimed as a right, but extensions for any period up to two years' absence from duty in all may be granted on medical certificate or on special or urgent grounds, at the discretion of the sanctioning authority. The previous assent of the authority under whom the individual may be serving will be required for such extensions, except on medical certificate, and must be obtained by the applicant. Extensions beyond two years' absence from duty will only be admissible in very special cases, and without any pay whatever.

V. Leave in or out of India will reckon as

service for pension, and will not involve forfeiture of appointment.

VI. Leave will commence on the date of being struck off duty, except that in the case of an officer having to leave his own station to appear before a Medical Board at another station, the date of being struck off duty will mean the date on which he quits the station where he is examined and invalidated. In cases of leave out of India on medical certificate the period of leave recommended by a Medical Board as necessary will be held to commence on the date of quitting India; the interval between being struck off duty and quitting India will be leave in India. In all cases, however, an officer will be due to rejoin his appointment at the expiry of the period granted to him, the day on which he rejoins it being reckoned as a day of duty.

VII. A deputy commissary, assistant commissary, deputy assistant commissary, senior assistant-surgeon, or warrant officer unable on account of ill health to return to his duty within the maximum period of two years' absence will, unless specially granted leave without pay, be placed on temporary half pay, or on the retired list, according to the circumstances of his case. An undue amount of leave on account of ill health will likewise render an officer liable to be transferred to half-pay or to the retired list, and the power of such transfer will be exercised by the Government of the Presidency or the Secretary of State for India, as the case may be.

VIII. A deputy commissary, assistant commissary, deputy assistant commissary, senior assistant-surgeon, or warrant officer on leave in or out of India will be required to rejoin his appointment at once on being recalled to duty on account of the exigencies of the service, unless pronounced by a Medical Board to be unfit to do so. He will be granted a free passage or not, according to the transport regulations in force at the time.

IX. When it may be necessary to convert sterling rates of pay under these rules into rupee currency, or vice versa, the conversion will be made at the rate of exchange in force for the period when the pay becomes due, as fixed annually for the adjustment of financial transactions between the Indian and Imperial Governments.

X. The rules which govern the allowances of deputy commissaries, assistant commissaries, deputy assistant commissaries, senior assistant-surgeons, and warrant officers acting for absentees remain unchanged.

XI. Advances of pay on proceeding on, or returning from, leave will be granted in accordance with the rules laid down in Army Regulations, India, Vol. I, Part I.

XII. The Secretary of State for India in Council reserves the right of making any change in the foregoing rules which circumstances may require.

CONDITIONS OF SERVICE ON TEMPORARY HALF-PAY.

APPLYING TO DEPUTY COMMISSARIES, ASSISTANT COMMISSARIES, DEPUTY ASSISTANT COMMISSARIES, SENIOR-ASSISTANT SURGEONS AND WARRANT OFFICERS, MEDICAL AND DEPARTMENTAL.

A member of any of the above classes when transferred to temporary half-pay may be permitted to remain on half-pay till his total continuous absence from duty, on furlough and half-pay together, amounts to four years. If

then still unfit to return to duty, he must be pensioned on the rate of pension for which he had qualified at the time of being placed on half-pay.

No period which he spends on half-pay will

count as service for Indian pension, nor will he be eligible for promotion while he is on half-pay. When replaced on the effective list, on return to duty from half-pay, he will be re-employed at once, being borne as a supernumerary in his grade until absorbed by the next vacancy.

He will be required to appear before a Medical Board at the expiration of one year on half-pay, and again, if retained on the half-pay list, at the end of the second year; and if, in view to being restored to the effective list, he should wish to appear before the Board at other times than those specified, he may be permitted to do so. When ordered periodically to appear before the Board he will be allowed, in this country, his bonâ fide travelling expenses, and lodging allowance at the rates stated below, but if appearing at other times, and at his own wish, he must do so at his own expense.

Rates of Lodging Allowance.

Deputy Commissary ... } 2s. 3d. per diem.
Senior Assistant Surgeon, with honorary rank of Surgeon - Captain after three years' service as such ... ..
Assistant Commissary ... } 2s. per diem.
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... ..
Senior Assistant Surgeon, with honorary rank of Surgeon-Captain, under three years' service as such ... .. } 1s. 4d. per diem.
Senior Assistant Surgeon, with honorary rank of Surgeon-Lieutenant ...
Conductor ... .. } 1s. 4d. per diem.
Sub-Conductor ... ..
Medical Warrant Officers ... ..

PENSION REGULATIONS FOR DEPUTY COMMISSARIES, ASSISTANT COMMISSARIES, DEPUTY ASSISTANT COMMISSARIES, AND WARRANT OFFICERS.

(These Rules took effect from the 1st September, 1881.)

1. All Deputy Commissaries, Assistant Commissaries, Deputy Assistant Commissaries, or Warrant Officers on the Establishment on 16th Sept., 1881, are allowed the option of electing the old or the new scale of pensions at any time previous to retirement, and are permitted, on one occasion only after retirement, the privilege of reconsidering their past decision on this point. The distinction between the invalid pension, which can be drawn only in India, and the retiring pension is abolished.

2. The periods of service entitling Deputy Commissaries, Assistant Commissaries, Deputy Assistant Commissaries or Warrant Officers to retiring pensions are as follows :-

  • 24 years' total service in Europe and India, including 16 years in the department.
  • 26 years' total service in Europe and India, including 14 years in the department.
  • 28 years' total service in Europe and India, including 12 years in the department.

3. The retiring pensions are—

England or the Colonies. India. Per annum. Per mensem.
Deputy Commissary ... £216 Rs. 200
Assistant Commissary ... 144 ... 140
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... .. 108 ... 100
Conductor ... .. 90 ... 85
Sub-Conductor ... .. 72 ... 70

4. A Deputy Commissary, Assistant Commissary, Deputy Assistant Commissary, or Warrant Officer of any grade, wishing to retire after 21 years' total service, receives a pension of £54 per annum in England or the Colonies, or Rs. 50 per mensem in India.

5. A Deputy Commissary, Assistant Commissary, Deputy Assistant Commissary, or Warrant Officer declared by a Medical Board to be unfit for further service in India, or compulsorily retired on superannuation after comple-

tion of half the periods of service, army and departmental, laid down in paragraph 2, receives—

England or the Colonies. Per annum. India. Per mensem.
Deputy Commissary ... £172 Rs. 160
Assistant Commissary ... 115 110
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... .. 86 80
Conductor ... .. 72 68
Sub-Conductor ... .. 58 56

6. If he has served less than half the prescribed periods, but more than 14 years altogether, he receives—

England or the Colonies. Per annum. India. Per mensem.
Deputy Commissary ... £115 Rs. 110
Assistant Commissary ... 86 80
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... .. 72 68
Conductor ... .. 58 56
Sub-Conductor ... .. 43 40

7. If he has not served 14 years altogether, his case will be specially considered.

8. A Deputy Commissary, Assistant Commissary, Deputy Assistant Commissary, or Warrant Officer, whose disqualification for further service in India has been caused by intemperance or irregular habits, receives, if he has served half the periods of service, army and departmental, laid down in paragraph 2—

England or the Colonies. Per annum. India. Per mensem.
Deputy Commissary ... £130 Rs. 120
Assistant Commissary ... 86 85
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... .. 65 60
Conductor ... .. 54 50
Sub-Conductor ... .. 43 42

9. If he has served less than half the prescribed periods, but more than 14 years altogether, he receives—

England or the Colonies. India.
Per annum. Per mensem.
Deputy Commissary ... £86 ...Rs. 85
Assistant Commissary 65 ... 60
Deputy Assistant Commissary ... 54 ... 50
Conductor ... 43 ... 42
Sub-Conductor ... 32 ... 30

10. If he has not served 14 years altogether, his case will be specially considered.

11. Retirement is compulsory at the age of 55 years.

12. All Warrant Officers on the establishment on 16th September, 1881, will be allowed the option of electing the old or the new scale of pensions at any time previous to retirement.

13. Pension, according to the present scale and conditions, is admissible to the widow of a Warrant Officer who may die, either while in the service or on the Pension establishment, provided, in the latter case, that she married the deceased Warrant Officer previous to his retiring from the army or being invalidated.

ROYAL INDIAN MARINE.

PARTICULARS RELATING TO FIRST APPOINTMENTS.

All first appointments of Officers and Engineers in the Royal Indian Marine are made by the Secretary of State for India in Council.

The limits of age for appointment to the junior executive rank of Sub-Lieutenant, are 17 and 22 years, and no candidate will be appointed who does not possess a Board of Trade certificate as Second Mate.

An application for appointment must be made on the prescribed form, which can be procured from the India Office. It must be accompanied by the candidate's Board of Trade certificate, and by certificates of his birth and character, which will be returned after being noted. It will be recorded, but will not be submitted for the orders of the Secretary of State until there is a vacancy.

The selection of candidates is not affected by absence from England.

A candidate must pass an examination by the Medical Board prior to appointment.

On appointment a first-class passage to Bombay will be provided, and, if required, an advance of

pay, not exceeding £25, recoverable in India, will be made in aid of outfit.

Pay commences from date of embarkation for India.

An officer's service for pension reckons from date of joining his ship or appointment if he is 19 years of age or over; otherwise from date of attaining that age.

As a condition of their appointment to the Royal Indian Marine, Officers are required to subscribe, from date of arrival in India, under the Indian Military Service Family Pension Regulations for the benefits of family pensions.

The names of candidates will be removed from the list on their attaining the age of 22 years.

Applications for appointment, or for further information should be addressed to the Military Secretary, India Office.

For conditions of appointment of Assistant Engineers, see page 285.

PAY AND ALLOWANCES.

The establishment of officers of the Royal Indian Marine, and their pay and allowances are as follows:—

25 Commanders ... On pay ranging from Rs. 350 to Rs. 500 per mensem, in addition to staff or command pay.
Lieutenants on completing six years' seniority ... On Rs. 250 per mensem.
Lieutenants on completing three years' seniority ... On Rs. 200 per mensem.
Lieutenants under three years' seniority ... On Rs. 150 per mensem.
19 Sub-Lieutenants On Rs. 125 per mensem.
13 Sub-Lieutenants On Rs. 100 per mensem.
Total - 89

This establishment is subject to such increase or decrease in any grades as may be required by any addition to, or reduction in, the vessels of the Royal Indian Marine.

COMMAND ALLOWANCES.

For the command of a sea-going ship in commission:— Rs. A. per
Over 300 and under 500 tons (gross) 20 diem.
" 500 " 1,000 " " 3 8 "
" 1,000 " 1,500 " " 5 0 "
" 1,500 " 2,000 " " 7 0 "
" 2,000 " — " " 10 0 "
For the command of a harbour ship in commission, or a sea-going ship in reserve, or out of commission, when especially appointed to command—half the above rates.
For the command of a river steamer:—
Over 200 and under 300 tons (gross) 2 0 "
" 300 " — " " 3 8 "
For the command of a small vessel not a tender to another ship, and not coming under any of the above denominations, and when the officer specially appointed to command ... 1 0 "

CHARGE ALLOWANCES.

For the senior officer of any vessel over 1,000 tons, when doing executive duty as such, and having not less than three years' service as a R. A. per Lieutenant, in addition to his pay 2 0 diem.

For the senior officer of any vessel under 1,000 tons, when doing executive duty as such, and having not less than three years' service as a R. A. per Lieutenant, in addition to his pay... 1 0 diem.

APPOINTMENTS RESERVED FOR OFFICERS OF THE ROYAL INDIAN MARINE.

2. A certain number of Shore, Port, and Marine Survey appointments are usually reserved for officers of the Royal Indian Marine. The numbers so reserved, and the allowances attached (in addition to pay of grade), are as follows:—

No. Rs.
4 Shore appointments 400—1000 per mensem.
11 Port appointments 320—870 "
10 Marine Survey 4—8 per diem.

For Leave and Pension Rules of the Royal Indian Marine, see pages 294 and 301.

ESTABLISHMENT AND PAY OF ENGINEERS AND ASSISTANT ENGINEERS OF THE ROYAL INDIAN MARINE.

1. The following is the sanctioned establishment of Engineers for the Royal Indian Marine serving afloat, with the several rates of pay:—

No. Grades. Rates of pay per mensem.
6 Chief Engineers... { 3 on Rs. 400.
49 Engineers ... ... } 3 on Rs. 300.
On Rs. 175, rising to Rs. 250, according length of approved service.
Assistant Engineers On Rs. 125 and Rs. 150

2. There is no limit to the number in the grade of Engineer so long as the sanctioned Establishment for the two grades of Engineer and Assistant Engineer is not exceeded.

3. In addition to these rates of pay, Chief Engineers, Engineers, and Assistant Engineers, when in charge of engines of ships in commission, will be allowed—

When in charge of engines of— Rs. A.
500 and under 750 I.H.P.... 1 8 per diem.
750 " 1,000 " ... 1 12 "
1,000 " 1,500 " ... 2 0 "
1,500 " 2,000 " ... 2 8 "
2,000 " 2,500 " ... 3 8 "
2,500 and over ... 5 0 "
When in charge of engines under 500 I.H.P. or of ships in reserve or out of commission, or of the yard craft establishment... 1 0 "

4. The above-mentioned allowance will in each case be drawn by the Engineer permanently appointed to the charge of the engines, and it will be continued to him during privilege leave, or when detached by competent authority on any temporary duty not exceeding twenty-eight days when no deputation or other allowances are drawn, and also during absence off duty in sick quarters for any period not exceeding forty-two days. In such cases no allowance is admissible to the Engineer appointed to the temporary charge of the engines.

5. Officers of the Engineer Establishment drawing charge allowance under the foregoing rules will also draw rations, or compensation in lieu, under existing rules.

6. The Director of the Royal Indian Marine is authorised to detail officers of the Engineer Establishment of the Indian Marine for temporary service for other duties whenever he considers it necessary to do so.

7. Assistant Engineers joining without certificates are entered on Rs. 125 per mensem,

and on obtaining certificates of competency as 2nd-class Engineers, either from the Board of Trade, or from a Colony, or from one of the Local Government Boards in India, are advanced to Rs. 150 per mensem. They remain on this scale for three years of approved service after obtaining certificates of competency as 1st-class Engineers before they become entitled to promotion to the grade of Engineer.

8. Assistant Engineers joining with certificates of competency as 2nd-class Engineers, either from the Board of Trade, or from a Colony, or from one of the Local Government Boards in India, are entered on Rs. 150 per mensem. They remain on this scale for three years of approved service after obtaining certificates of competency as 1st-class Engineers before they become entitled to promotion to the grade of Engineer.

9. Assistant Engineers joining with a 1st-class certificate of competency, either from the Board of Trade, or from a Colony, or from one of the Local Government Boards in India, are entered on Rs. 150 per mensem, and are entitled to promotion to the grade of Engineer after three years' approved service.

10. Engineers will be entitled to the following rates of pay:—

After 3 years' approved service as Engineers, Rs. 200 per mensem.

After 5 years' approved service as Engineers, Rs. 225 per mensem.

After 7 years' approved service as Engineers, Rs. 250 per mensem.

11. The sanction of Government is necessary to the grant of the higher rate of pay to Chief Engineers.

QUALIFICATIONS REQUIRED FOR APPOINTMENT AND PROMOTION.

For Assistant Engineer.

12. The candidate must not be less than 18 nor more than 25 years of age, and he must produce certificates of service in a steam factory for at least four years. If not in possession of a certificate of competency as a 2nd-class or 1st-class Engineer, he will, if in India, be examined by the Inspector of Machinery of the dockyard and a Chief or other senior Engineer, and such other person as the Director or Deputy Director of the Royal Indian Marine may think fit to select, as to his ability as a workman at the several trades a knowledge of which is requisite for the performance of his duties; he will also be examined in the practical use of the various instruments employed in the engine-room, and in the duties generally of a Marine Engineer; and no candidate will be entered unless the examining Officers certify that his skill as a

workman, his knowledge of machinery, and his good and steady conduct fully qualify him for the appointment.

13. On completion of the sea time required by the Board of Trade, Assistant Engineers of the Royal Indian Marine will obtain the permission of their commanding officers to present themselves for examination, and report the result thereof to them for the information of the Director of the Royal Indian Marine. Officers in command of the Royal Indian Marine vessels will see that the Assistant Engineers under their orders present themselves for examination for certificates of competency as 2nd and 1st class Engineers, as soon as they complete their sea time, and will submit to the Director of the Royal Indian Marine a report containing any explanation that may be necessary, of every instance in which an Assistant Engineer on board his vessel neglects to be examined in proper time. In the event of an Assistant Engineer failing to present himself for examination within twelve months after completing the necessary sea service, and being unable to furnish a satisfactory explanation, or in the event of his attaining the age of 27 years without obtaining a Board of Trade certificate of competency as a 1st class Engineer, a report on his case will be submitted to the Government of India, in order that it may be considered whether his further retention in the service is desirable. Failure on the second trial to obtain a certificate as 2nd or 1st class Engineer will involve a loss of three months' seniority; and should an Assistant Engineer fail a third time, his case will be submitted for the orders of the Government of India.

Engineer.

14. To qualify for appointment as an Engineer,

an Officer must have served as an Assistant Engineer on Rs. 150 per mensem in an Indian Government vessel for three years, and be in possession of a certificate of competency as a 1st-class Engineer either from the Board of Trade, or from a Colony, or from one of the Local Government Boards in India.

15. The Government of India reserve the right which they have always exercised of admitting Officers from other services or departments to any class of the establishment or of making promotions on public grounds irrespective of seniority.

16. Promotions to the grade of Chief Engineer will be made wholly by selection, by the Government of India, on the recommendation of the Director of the Royal Indian Marine. Chief Engineers will be promoted to the Rs. 400 grade only after twenty years' service.

17. When certificated and uncertificated Engineers are serving on board the same vessel, the certificated Engineers will take precedence above the uncertificated Engineers.

18. A ship's Engineer transferred to a dock-yard appointment will be seconded, and a promotion made in his place. If brought back again on the establishment, he will be borne as supernumerary till absorbed. Officers temporarily transferred will not be seconded; but acting promotions will be made in such cases, and in the room of Officers absent on furlough or leave.

NOTE.—In paragraphs 7, 8, 9, and 14, the certificates of competency referred to as being obtained from one of the Local Government Boards in India are those equivalent to the Board of Trade or Colonial Certificates, and do not include those known as "First-class Local Indian Home Trade Certificates."

FURLOUGH AND LEAVE RULES OF THE ROYAL INDIAN MARINE.

1884 RULES.

Furlough.

I.—Officers after five years' service in India or under the Government of India, exclusive of all periods of absence on furlough, may receive one year's furlough, and an additional year for each subsequent five years' service. Furlough not taken at the time it becomes due is not lost, and can, subject to these Regulations, be taken later.

II.—An officer may not be absent from duty on any grounds for more than three years consecutively, nor on return from furlough may he receive, except under Rule VII., any further furlough until after an interval of three years.

III.—Furlough may be spent anywhere, and when it has been earned by length of service, counts as service for pension. Furlough taken before it is earned by length of service reckons against service for pension till paid off.

IV.—An officer on furlough in receipt of the rate of pay in force before 1st April, 1886, receives half the pay of his rank, or if holding a shore appointment, half the pay of his appoint-

ment, excluding command, charge, acting, personal, or house allowance, computed according to a rate of exchange to be fixed in each year by the Secretary of State. An officer on the new rates of pay will draw, when on furlough, half the pay of his grade plus half the staff allowance of his appointment. An officer promoted while on furlough will be entitled to a moiety of the increased pay due to that promotion.

V.—An officer proceeding on or returning from furlough may be granted an advance of three months' furlough pay; and he may further* be granted the advance in aid of passage money specified in clause IX. if he does not obtain passage by troopship. Advances made in England will be recovered in India by monthly installments of one-fourth of the pay drawn by the officers, which will include all permanent or temporary allowances drawn by them. This recovery will be made at the official rate of exchange fixed for the year in which the advance is made. Advances made in India will be noted on the last pay certificates of officers, and no

* Unless holding shore appointments of Rs. 1,000 per mensem. Such officers are ineligible for passage by troopship (cl. ix.) and are also ineligible for any advance exceeding three months' Furlough pay. Marine Circular, No. 1 of 1888.

issue of furlough pay will be made till after the sum advanced has accrued as furlough pay.

VI.—Furlough pay may not be drawn for more than two years consecutively.

VII.—Upon certificate of a Medical Board, an officer not entitled to furlough, who has served not less than one year in India, may be granted furlough. Such furlough will not count of service for pension until it has been paid off by subsequent service under the terms of Rule I. Furlough cannot be anticipated beyond two years in the aggregate, nor for a second term, unless that previously anticipated has been subsequently paid off under the terms of Rule I.

VIII.—An officer who has exhausted the furlough obtainable under these Rules, and who may be certified by a Medical Board to be still unfit for duty, will be retired on the pension or gratuity to which he may be entitled; if not entitled to either pension or gratuity, his case will be specially considered (but see under heading "Temporary Retirement," on p. 292).

IX.—Marine officers not holding shore appointments of Rs. 1,000 per mensem will be eligible for the grant of non-regulation passages in troopships when such passages are available. Officers who do not obtain passages in troopships will be allowed advances* in aid of passage money, in addition to the advances admissible under Clause V., provided that no officer shall in any case draw advances exceeding three months' pay in all.

X.—Furlough, whether on private affairs or on medical certificate, is restricted to a maximum period of one year in the first instance, and to subsequent extensions not exceeding six months at a time.

General Leave.

XI.—General leave may be granted for any period not exceeding six months at a time, but cannot be renewed, except for very urgent reasons, till after two years have elapsed from its expiration. It may be spent anywhere, does not involve forfeiture of appointment, and counts as service for pension. It will be granted or refused entirely at the discretion of the Director of the Indian Marine. It may be granted up to a maximum limit of 30 days as subsidiary to furlough, whether on proceeding on furlough or on return from furlough. Leave granted as subsidiary to furlough can only be spent in India. The grant of furlough out of India carries with it the grant of the subsidiary leave admissible under this clause.

XII.—During general leave an officer will be restricted to the pay of his grade,† and will forfeit all allowances, which will be paid to his locum tenens. If an officer exceeds the six months' leave admissible at one time under this Rule, the entire period will be converted into furlough, and his allowances adjusted accordingly. The advances of pay sanctioned under Rule V. are not admissible to officers proceeding on or returning from general leave.

Privilege Leave.

XIII.—Subject to the exigencies of the service, Marine officers may be allowed 60 days' privilege leave during each year, and each leave may be taken in installments.

XIV.—An officer who has privilege leave to his credit when proceeding on leave or furlough does not forfeit it, but privilege leave cannot be taken in continuation of furlough, nor can furlough be obtained in continuation of privilege leave. Privilege leave may, however, be converted into general leave with the sanction of the Director of the Indian Marine, or into furlough with the sanction of the Government of India.

XV.—Privilege leave will be granted by the Director of the Indian Marine, but it will be granted only on the understanding that no additional expense is thereby caused to the State, either directly or indirectly, that the applicant's services can be spared, and that satisfactory arrangements for the performance of his duties during his absence can be made.

XVI.—Officers are at liberty to proceed wherever they may desire when on privilege leave, provided they can ensure their return before the expiration of the leave. If an officer exceeds his privilege leave, the whole period of his absence will be converted into general leave or furlough, and his allowances and statement of service for furlough adjusted accordingly.

Temporary Leave.

XVII.—Temporary leave not exceeding 10 days, and not in anticipation or extension of any kind of leave or furlough, may be granted by the Director and Deputy Director of the Indian Marine.

XVIII.—Temporary leave not exceeding 72 hours may be granted by commanders of vessels and heads of departments to officers under their command.

General Regulations.

XIX.—It must be distinctly understood that furlough or leave cannot be claimed as a right. Both are subject to the exigencies of the public service, and will be granted only when an officer's services can be spared. Commanders of vessels and heads of departments will not forward an application for furlough or leave unless they are able to satisfy the Director of the Indian Marine that the officer's services can be spared without any inconvenience to the public service. An officer who has not completed his term of probation for the Marine Service, cannot be granted leave with retention of his probationary appointment in excess of three months.

XX.—No grant of leave given under the conditions of these Rules will count against an officer's service for pension, and leave may be spent anywhere, i.e., within possible limits of return before it expires.

XXI.—Leave is reckoned from the day on which an officer avails himself of it, and it expires on the day he rejoins.

Furlough reckons from date of leaving India, or from date of leaving his ship if at an outstation, unless specially stated by authority to commence on an earlier date, and ceases on the day of return to India.

The day of return from leave is a day of duty; also that of return from furlough, where duty commences at port of disembarkation.

XXII.—Officers on furlough in Europe will draw their pay from the India Office, but officers

* See footnote * preceding page.

† This Rule will also hold good in the case of an officer holding a shore appointment, in which case the balance will be considered a staff allowance payable to the locum tenens provided the latter does not thereby draw more than the pay of the appointment.

on general or privilege leave must arrange to draw their pay in India.

XXIII.—As a rule leave will be granted only during the period of the south-west monsoon.

XXIV.—Officers proceeding on furlough will report their departure personally at the office of the Director or Deputy Director of the Indian Marine, except when they avail themselves of it at an out-station. An officer returning from furlough or leave will rejoin the ship to which he belongs; but if this is impracticable, and he has not received instructions (which he should apply for beforehand), he will rejoin at Bombay or Calcutta, reporting himself in person to the Director or Deputy Director. On the departure of an officer on furlough or leave, or on the return of an officer therefrom, the commander of the vessel will cause an entry to be made of the date in the ship's log-book, and will forward an extract thereof by the next post to the Director. Except when orders are sent to the contrary, all officers returning from furlough will report themselves to the Director of the Royal Indian Marine at Bombay.

XXV.—Officers arriving in Europe on furlough or on general leave must report their arrival in writing to the Military Secretary at the India Office, and state their address; and for extension of furlough or for permission to return to duty, they must also apply to the Military Secretary. Before applying for extension of furlough on private affairs they must obtain permission to do so from the Government of India, through the Director of Marine. The permission thus obtained must be attached to the application for extension, and submitted to the Military Secretary at the India Office.

Officers proceeding to the colonies on furlough should apply to India for extension of furlough and for permission to return to duty.

XXVI.—During the absence of an officer on leave or furlough, he must keep the Director of the Royal Indian Marine acquainted with his actual address, and be prepared to return immediately to India, should his services be required. When on leave, such recall order will be issued by the Director; when on furlough in Europe, by the Secretary of State. Officers spending their furlough in Europe must keep the Secretary of State informed of their address.

XXVII.—Proceedings of Medical Boards on officers of the R.I.M. invalided to Europe should state—

  1. I.—Whether the illness is the result of climate, and caused in and by the service.
  2. II.—Whether change of climate in or out of India is necessary.
  3. III.—The period of absence recommended in or out of India.

XXVIII.—No officer on leave or furlough may take service under any other employer in India without special sanction.

NOTE.—

The above rules are applicable to—

  1. I.—Officers on the Graded and Super-numerary List of Executive Officers.
  2. II.—Officers on the Graded List of Engineers.
  3. III.—Gazetted clerks,
  4. IV.—Warrant officers who may be specially admitted to the rules.

1892 RULES.

1. These rules shall apply to—

  1. (a) All officers appointed to the Indian Marine after 26 April, 1892.
  2. (b) Officers who elected them within one year of the date of their publication.

NOTE.—The term "officers" includes the Graded and Super-numerary List of Executive Officers and the Graded List of Engineers.

2. Those who apply for leave of any kind within one year of the date of publication of these rules must make their election when submitting their applications for leave; all others, whether on duty or on furlough or leave on 26 April, 1892, may elect at convenience within the specified time.

3. All officers who elect these rules will come under their provisions from the date of the receipt of their applications by the Government of India if they are in India, or at the India Office if they are at home; and they will be entitled to count all past furlough and leave as service for pension under Rule V.

4. Those who elect these rules while on furlough or general leave under the previous regulations will be allowed the option of continuing to draw any furlough or leave allowances they may be in receipt of under those regulations, if more advantageous, until the expiry of such furlough or general leave or any extension thereof which would have been admissible under the previous regulations, subject to the limit of two years' absence from duty.

5. It must be clearly understood that officers who do not elect these rules within the period stated will be held to have declined them, and will be allowed no further opportunity of electing them.

6. "General leave" and "subsidiary leave" will not be granted to officers under these rules.

Rules.

I.—An officer subject to these rules may be granted—

  1. (a) Privilege leave and temporary leave under such regulations as may from time to time be in force.
  2. (b) Leave in or out of India for one year, capable of extension to two years' absence from duty under conditions stated below.

Leave in or out of India other than privilege or temporary leave.

II.—During leave in or out of India other than privilege or temporary leave, the following rates of pay will be drawn, whether an officer holds a shore appointment or not :—

Rs. per mensem.
Commanders, 1st Grade... 410
" 2nd Grade ... 330
" 3rd Grade ... 280
Lieutenants of six years' seniority ... 210
" " of three " ... 170
" " under ditto ... 130
Rs. per
mensem.
Sub-Lieutenants on Rs. 125 per mensem 120
Sub-Lieutenants on Rs. 100 per mensem 110
Chief Engineers on Rs. 400 per mensem 320
" " on Rs. 300 per mensem 270
Engineers on Rs. 250 230
" on Rs. 225 215
" on Rs. 200 200
" on Rs. 175 190
Assistant Engineers on Rs. 150 165
" " on Rs. 125 150

An officer promoted while on leave will be entitled to the higher rate of leave pay from date of promotion.

III.—Leave may be granted at any time at the discretion of the Government of India, on the recommendation of the Director of the Royal Indian Marine. Leave in or out of India not exceeding six months may be granted by the Director of the Royal Indian Marine without previous reference to the Government of India, a report of such leave being made for notification in Government General Orders.

IV.—Extensions of leave in or out of India in excess of one year, but not exceeding two years' absence from duty in all, may be granted on medical certificate or on special or urgent grounds, at the discretion of the sanctioning authority. The previous assent of the Government of India will be required for extensions, except on medical certificate, and must be obtained by the applicant through the Director of the Royal Indian Marine. Extensions beyond two years' absence from duty will only be admissible in very special cases, and without any pay whatever.

V.—Leave in or out of India will reckon as service for pension.

VI.—Leave will commence on the date of being struck off duty, except that in the case of an officer having to appear before a Medical Board the date of being struck off duty will mean the date on which he quits the station where he is examined and invalidated. In cases of leave out of India on medical certificate the period of leave recommended by a Medical Board as necessary will be held to commence on the date of quitting India. In all cases, however, an officer will be due to rejoin his ship or appointment at the expiry of the period granted to him, the day on which he rejoins it being reckoned as a day of duty.

VII.—An officer unable on account of ill health to return to his duty within the maximum period of two years' absence will, unless specially granted leave without pay, be placed on temporary or permanent retirement, according to the circumstances of his case. An undue amount of leave on account of ill health will likewise render an officer liable to be transferred to temporary or permanent retirement.

VIII.—An officer on leave in or out of India will be required to rejoin his appointment at once on being recalled to duty on account of the exigencies of the service, unless pronounced by a Medical Board to be unfit to do so.

IX.—An officer proceeding on leave will report his departure personally at the office of the Director or Deputy Director of the Royal Indian

Marine, except when he avails himself of it at an out-station. Except when ordered to the contrary, all officers returning from leave will report themselves to the Director of the Royal Indian Marine at Bombay.

An officer arriving in Europe on leave must report his arrival in writing to the Military Secretary at the India Office, and state his address; and for extension of leave or for permission to return to duty, he must also apply to the Military Secretary. An officer applying for extension of leave on private affairs must, except in very special or urgent circumstances, attach an intimation of the assent of the Government of India.

An officer proceeding to the Colonies on leave should apply to India for extensions of leave and for permission to return to duty.

X.—Leave pay under these rules, when issued out of India, will be converted into sterling at the official rate of exchange in force for the period when the pay becomes due, as fixed annually for the adjustment of financial transactions between the India and Imperial Governments.

XI.—Advances not exceeding three months' full pay of grade, subject to a limit of 100l. to Commanders, and 65l. to officers of junior rank, will be allowed on proceeding on or returning from leave, and on returning from temporary retirement. Advances made in England will be recovered in India by monthly instalments of one-fourth of the pay drawn by the officers, which will include all permanent or temporary allowances drawn by them. This recovery will be made at the official rate of exchange fixed for the year in which the advance is made. Advances made in India will be noted on the last pay certificates of officers, and no issue of leave pay will be made till after the sum advanced has accrued as such. In case of death no recovery of advances made under this rule is to be effected from the estate of the deceased officer.

XII.—Marine officers not holding shore appointments of 1,000 rupees per mensem will be eligible for the grant of non-regulation passages in troopships when such passages are available.

Privileges and Temporary Leave under these Rules.

XIII.—Subject to the exigencies of the service, Marine officers may be allowed 60 days' privilege leave during each year, and such leave may be taken in instalments.

XIV.—Accumulated privilege leave for 90 days may be granted to an officer who has no privilege leave during the preceding 33 months, but no "leave in or out of India," if exceeding six months, can be counted as part of the fixed 33 months.

XV.—An officer who has privilege leave (ordinary or accumulated) at his credit, when proceeding on "leave in or out of India," does not forfeit it, but privilege leave cannot be taken in continuation of "leave in or out of India," nor can "leave in or out of India" be taken in continuation of privilege leave.

Privilege leave may, however, be converted into "leave in or out of India" with the sanction of the Government of India.

XVI.—An officer returning from accumulated privilege leave granted in one year and extending into the next is eligible for ordinary privilege leave during the period when such leave is valid.

able in the year in which he returns, provided he rejoins before proceeding on ordinary privilege leave.

XVII.—Privilege leave will be granted by the Director of the Royal Indian Marine, but only on the understanding that no additional expense is thereby caused to the State either directly or indirectly, that the applicant's services can be spared, and that satisfactory arrangements for the performance of his duties during his absence can be made.

XVIII.—Privilege leave (ordinary or accumulated) reckons as service for pension.

XIX.—As a rule privilege leave will only be granted during the period of the south-west monsoon.

XX.—Officers are at liberty to proceed wherever they may desire when on privilege leave, provided they can ensure their return before the expiration of the leave. If an officer exceeds his privilege leave the whole period of his absence will be converted into "leave in or out of India," and his allowances and statement of service for "leave in or out of India" adjusted accordingly.

XXI.—Privilege leave is reckoned from the day on which an officer avails himself of it, and it expires on the day he rejoins, the day of return being reckoned as a day of duty.

XXII.—An officer of the floating establishment returning from privilege leave will, if possible, rejoin the ship to which he belongs, but if this is impracticable, and he has not received instructions (which he should apply for beforehand), he will rejoin at Bombay or Calcutta, reporting himself in person to the Director or Deputy Director. On the departure of an officer on privilege leave or on the return of an officer therefrom, the Commander of the vessel will cause an entry to be made of the date in the ship's log, and will forward an extract thereof by the next post to the Director.

XXIII.—Temporary leave not exceeding 10 days and not in anticipation or in extension of any other kind of leave, may be granted by the Director and Deputy Director of the Royal Indian Marine.

XXIV.—Temporary leave not exceeding 72 hours may be granted by Commanders of vessels and heads of departments to officers under their command.

General Regulations.

XXV.—It must be distinctly understood that leave in or out of India, or privilege leave, cannot be claimed as a right. Both are subject to the exigencies of the public service, and will be granted only when an officer's services can be spared. Commanders of vessels and heads of

departments will not forward an application for privilege or other leave unless they are able to satisfy the Director of the Royal Indian Marine that the officer's services can be spared without any inconvenience to the public service.

XXVI.—An officer who has not completed his term of probation for the Marine service cannot be granted leave with retention of his probationary appointment in excess of three months.

XXVII.—The proceedings of Medical Boards on officers of the Royal Indian Marine should state:—

  1. (1) Whether the illness is the result of climate, and caused in and by the service.
  2. (2) Whether change of climate in or out of India is necessary.
  3. (3) The period of absence recommended in or out of India.

XXVIII.—The name of an officer granted leave out of India (other than privilege leave), will be removed from the ship on which borne, and he will draw his pay during such leave—

  1. (a) if proceeded to Europe, through the India Office.
  2. (b) if proceeded to any colony named in the list in Appendix No. 17 of the Civil Service Regulations, in such colony.

He can, however, draw his leave pay in India if he prefers it.

XXIX.—The name of an officer, granted leave in India (other than privilege leave), will be removed from the ship on which borne, and he will draw his pay during such time from any treasury in India in communication with the Examiner of Marine Accounts.

XXX.—The name of an officer, granted privilege leave (ordinary or accumulated), will continue on the books of his ship, and he will draw his pay either from the ship or from any treasury in India or through a bank or agent, as may be arranged through the Examiner of Marine Accounts.

XXXI.—During the absence of an officer on leave (privilege or otherwise), he must keep the Director of the Royal Indian Marine acquainted with his actual address and be prepared to return immediately to India, should his services be required. When on privilege leave, such recall order will be issued by the Director; when on leave in Europe (other than privilege), by the Secretary of State. Officers spending their leave (other than privilege) in Europe must keep the Secretary of State informed of their address.

XXXII.—The Secretary of State in Council reserves to himself the right of making any change in the foregoing rules which circumstances may require.

MARINE CIRCULAR No. 13,

DATED 6TH OCTOBER, 1891

(As revised by Marine Circular No. 3, dated 1st August, 1893).

Rules for the Instruction in England of Officers of the Royal Indian Marine in Gunnery and the use of Torpedoes, and in other scientific branches of their profession.

In supersession of Marine Circular No. 7, of the 27th April, 1891, the Governor-General in Council is pleased, with the approval of the Secretary of State for India, to notify the follow-

ing rules for the instruction in England of officers of the Royal Indian Marine in gunnery and the use of torpedoes, and in other scientific branches of their profession.

2. All officers and engineers of the Royal Indian Marine, whether in India or on leave or furlough in England, are at liberty to apply for permission to attend all courses and classes open to officers or engineers of the Royal Navy on full pay, including the courses at the Royal Naval College, Greenwich.

3. There will in future be no restriction on the number of officers or engineers undergoing instruction at the same time.

4. The courses are:—

For Officers—

(I.) Short course for Lieutenants, consisting of eight weeks' each of gunnery and torpedo.

(II.) The session of the Royal Naval College, Greenwich, for Captains, Commanders, Lieutenants, and Navigating Officers, which commences on the 1st October in each year, and lasts till the 30th June following.

For Engineers—

(I.) Course on board the "Vernon" and "Excellent;" the former beginning in January, March, and April, and lasting for 45 days each; the latter commencing in February, April, and June, and lasting for 25 days each.

(II.) A course at the Royal Naval College, Greenwich, which usually begins in October of each year.

5. Every officer or engineer permitted to attend a course will be on duty for the period he may be under instruction, and if on leave or furlough, except leave on medical certificate, such period will be reckoned as in addition to his leave or furlough.

6. Officers and engineers attending courses will be entitled to full grade pay, together with subsistence and travelling allowances for that period.

7. Officers and engineers failing to qualify, except on account of sickness, will, if on leave or furlough, lose all the advantages of being on duty during the period of instruction; that is to say, they will be restricted to their grade or furlough pay, as the case may be.

8. It is therefore to be understood that officers and engineers while under instruction will be granted their full pay, subject to adjustment under paragraph 7, but the subsistence allowance will be withheld until the termination of the course, as it will only be admissible to officers who pass.

9. Should the Government of India select an officer or engineer to undergo instruction at home, either on board the training ships or at the Royal Naval College, Greenwich, or elsewhere, or to go through a combined course at more than one centre of instruction, he will be given a free passage to England and back, and will be considered on duty while under instruction, and will also receive full pay and

subsistence and travelling allowances, subject to the operation of Rule 8.

10. If an officer so selected and sent to England at the public expense fails to obtain a certificate at the termination of the course, he will be placed on furlough pay for the period of his absence from India.

11. The cases of all officers prevented by sickness from completing a course of instruction will be considered as they arise.

12. In forwarding applications from officers on leave or furlough for permission to undergo instruction, the Director of the Royal Indian Marine should be careful to see that no infringement of the furlough regulations is likely to take place from the application being granted. No officer will be selected who has not sufficient leave unexpired at the commencement of the course to enable him to return to duty on its termination, or who cannot be granted an extension of leave under existing furlough rules.

13. The Director of the Royal Indian Marine should also satisfy himself that applicants are likely to qualify in the subject in which they desire to receive instruction. In the case of Engineer officers desirous of being admitted to study at the Royal Naval College, Greenwich, the Director will, before forwarding their applications, cause them to pass an examination based on that which has to be undergone by Engineer students of the Royal Navy.

14. Officers in future, when applying for permission to attend courses of instruction, should specify the particular course or courses which they desire to enter. Their applications should be forwarded to the Government of India in time to allow of the Secretary of State's sanction being obtained before the commencement of the course; and recommendations for permission to allow officers to undergo instruction should be made by the Director of the Royal Indian Marine as soon after their departure from India as possible, especially in the case of officers on general leave.

15. The subjects of examination at the Royal Naval College, Greenwich, will be—

I.—Mathematics;

II.—Navigation, theoretical and practical;

III.—Compass adjustment;

IV.—Steam;

V.—Physics, only magnetism (but with electricity for those passing to the "Vernon" for torpedo course);

VI.—Surveying;

and 40 per cent. of marks upon these subjects on the papers set to the senior officers will entitle officers to a qualifying certificate. If, further, an officer should obtain the honorary certificate, he will have the letter "G" placed against his name in the list of officers of the Royal Indian Marine.

16. Officers will be at liberty to take up meteorology, practical astronomy, or any other subject of the senior officer's course as optional subjects.

PAY AND ALLOWANCES OF OFFICERS, ENGINEERS, &c., OF THE ROYAL INDIAN MARINE when on Special duty in England.

MARINE CIRCULAR, No. 4, DATED 2ND MARCH, 1888.

It is notified that officers and engineers of the Indian Marine, on special duty in England will, on arrival in England, receive the full pay of their grade plus subsistence allowance.

MARINE CIRCULAR, No. 10, DATED 17TH MAY, 1888.

In continuation of Marine Circular No. 4 of 1888, the Governor-General in Council is pleased, with the sanction of Her Majesty's Government, to notify the following scales of subsistence and travelling allowances for Officers, Engineers, Warrant Officers and others of Her Majesty's Indian Marine, when on special duty in England:—

Subsistence Allowance.

Per diem.
s. d.

1st-Grade Officers and Chief Engineers 5 0
2nd-Grade Officers and Engineers } 3 6
3rd-Grade Officers and Assistant Engineers, and Warrant Officers...

Travelling Allowances.

Per diem.

Petty Officers and Seamen, when travelling singly ... .. } 3 0

2. In calculating the travelling allowances, the first and last days spent in travelling will only reckon as half-days, and this rule will also apply when the journey is completed in one day.

3. The allowance for provisions and lights, &c., viz., 1s. 6d. a day, which is included in the scale of subsistence, will be held in abeyance while the travelling allowance is being drawn, and the lodging money will only be admissible concurrently with the travelling allowance in the case of an officer producing a certificate showing that his lodgings were retained and paid for while he was travelling.

CONDITIONS OF TEMPORARY RETIREMENT OF OFFICERS OF THE ROYAL INDIAN MARINE.

(Sanctioned by Despatch to Government of India, No. 369, dated 16th December, 1886.)

Officers of the Royal Indian Marine, who, after exhausting the furlough admissible to them are unfit for service, but not irremedially broken in health, may be temporarily transferred to the retired list. Temporary retirement is subject to the condition that the disease which necessitates it must have been contracted in and by the service.

An officer granted temporary retirement may be permitted to remain in that position till his total continuous absence from duty, on furlough and temporary retirement together, amounts to four years. If then still unfit to return to duty, he must be placed on the permanent retired list, receiving the pension (if any) or gratuity for which he had qualified at the time of being granted temporary retirement.

No period spent on temporary retirement will count as service for pension, nor will an officer be eligible for promotion while he is in that position. An officer replaced on the effective list, on becoming fit to return to duty, will be restored to the grade from which he retired, and his name will be placed in the same numerical position in that grade which it occupied at the time of temporary retirement.

Officers will be required to appear before a Medical Board at the expiration of one year on temporary retirement, and again, if retained in that position, before the end of the second year; and those who, in view to being restored to the effective list, may wish to appear before the Board at other times than those specified, may be permitted to do so. Officers ordered periodically to appear before the Board will be allowed, in the United Kingdom, their bonâ fide travelling expenses, and lodging allowance at the rates stated below, but those appearing at other times, and at their own wish, must do so at their own expense.

Rates of Lodging Allowances.

s. d.
Commander ... .. 5 0 per diem.
Lieutenant ... .. 4 0 "
Sub-Lieutenant ... .. 3 0 "
Chief Engineer... .. 5 0 "
Engineer... .. 4 0 "
Assistant Engineer ... .. 3 0 "

PENSION REGULATIONS OF THE ROYAL INDIAN MARINE.
1885 RULES.

1. The following is the scale of pensions and compassionate allowances admissible to officers and engineers of Her Majesty's Indian Marine to their families.

2. The service for pension of an officer of the Marine reckons from date of joining his ship or appointment if he is 19 years of age or over; otherwise from date of attaining that age.

3. An officer admitted to the Service after 30 years of age will be subject to reduction of pension by one-fortieth for every year or part of a year by which his age at the commencement of his qualifying service exceeded 30 years.

4. Retirement at the age of 55 years will be compulsory, and all officers and engineers of that age now in the Service, as well as those who

may attain that age within one year, will be required to retire at the expiration of one year from the date of this order. The amount of superannuation pension is that which would be admissible as an invalid pension. (Marine Circular No. 8 of 1888.) Should an officer holding the appointment of Director or Deputy Director of the Indian Marine attain that age during his incumbency, he may, with the approval of the Government of India, complete five years in the appointment.

[This rule will not be applicable to those officers who have already received special extension of tenure.]

5. The following is the scale of pensions:—

Designation. Officers invalided after 3 and under 15 years' service, owing to disease contracted in and by the service. After 15 years' service, if invalided, and 20 years' without invaliding. After 25 years' service. After 30 years' service, inclusive of the periods of Furlough allowed to count as service for pension.
After 3 years' service. After 6 years' service. After 10 years' service.
Per diem. Per mensem.
Rs. A. P. Rs. A. P. Rs. A. P. Rs. Rs. Rs.
Commanders, 1st Grade ... .. 2 8 0 4 0 0 5 8 0 270 410 490
Commanders, 2nd Grade ... .. 220 330 400
Commanders, 3rd Grade ... .. 190 280 340 140 210 260
Lieutenants of six years' seniority ... .. 120 170 210
Lieutenants of three years' seniority ... .. 90 130 160 80 120 150
Sub-Lieutenants, late Second-Grade Officers ... .. 70 110 130
Sub-Lieutenants, late Third-Grade Officers ... .. 210 320 380 180 270 320
Chief Engineers on Rs. 400 per mensem ... .. 150 230 280
Chief Engineers on Rs. 300 per mensem ... .. 140 200 250 130 190 230

Engineers of four years' service in that grade ... .. 1 12 0 2 4 0 2 8 0
